reasoning: The core legislative vehicle for Republicans altering the IRA in 2025 is the budget reconciliation package, dubbed the 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act' (OBBBA). The House passed its version on May 22, 2025, and the Senate Finance Committee released its draft in mid-June. Searches of the explicit contents of both bills reveal that while Republicans are drastically overhauling the Section 48E Investment Tax Credit architecture, they are not taking the path of making the existing domestic content bonus requirements mandatory for the base credit. Instead, the bills aim to restrict 48E through immediate or severe phase-outs (beginning in 2026 or 2028), eliminating credit transferability, and imposing strict mandatory restrictions on sourcing from Foreign Entities of Concern (FEOC). While the Senate bill includes language to make the domestic content bonus 'more effective', industry analysts confirm that it remains a separate bonus mechanism layered on top of a sharply reduced, phasing-out base credit. Since the OBBBA is already far along in its legislative journey and there is no evidence of this specific, narrow proposal (making the domestic content bonus requirement mandatory for base 48E eligibility) gaining any traction in either chamber, the probability of it being enacted before 2026 is extremely low.
